title: Waymo DC forecast market drafts
warning: Draft only. No market has been created and no bet has been placed.

markets.question: Will Waymo be legally permitted to offer fully driverless paid robotaxi rides in Washington, DC by 2026-12-31?
markets.description_markdown:
## Context
Waymo says Waymo One is planned for Washington, DC in 2026, but also says it still needs policymakers to formalize regulations for operation without a human behind the wheel. Existing DC law establishes an autonomous vehicle testing program and requires DDOT approval for changes such as moving from an in-vehicle test operator to no test operator physically present. Axios reported in July 2025 that Waymo vehicles in DC were being tested with a specialist behind the wheel and that a Council bill would be needed before driverless testing and public rides.

## Resolution
Resolve YES if, by 2026-12-31, DC law, regulation, DDOT permit approval, or another official DC government action legally permits Waymo to offer fully driverless paid ride-hailing service to public riders in Washington, DC with no human driver or test operator physically present in the vehicle. Resolve NO otherwise. Testing-only permission without paid public rides does not count.

Resolution date target: 2026-12-31

## Sources
- [Waymo: Next stop for Waymo One: Washington, D.C.](https://waymo.com/blog/2025/03/next-stop-for-waymo-one-washingtondc/)
- [DC Law 23-156: Autonomous Vehicles Testing Program Amendment Act of 2020](https://code.dccouncil.gov/us/dc/council/laws/23-156)
- [Axios: New D.C. Council bill would allow Waymo to begin robotaxi service](https://www.axios.com/local/washington-dc/2025/07/16/driverless-cars-dc-bill-waymo-robotaxis)

## Seed forecast
- Point estimate: 52
- Rationale: Waymo publicly targeted DC riders in 2026 and has strong incentive to secure approval, but DC policy approval and public-service authorization remain live gating items.

_Drafted by farness. Review wording and resolution criteria before posting._

Resolve NO otherwise. Testing-only permission without paid public rides does not count.
